---
title: "Session 7: AI Tools II"
subtitle: "Agentic desktop AI (Cowork)"
author: "Prof. Ariel Ortiz-Bobea"
date: "April 29, 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../slides-theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    chalkboard: false
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    progress: true
    controls: true
    menu:
      side: left
    include-after-body: ../../slides-progress.html
---

## Pre-Class Checklist

[§ Tutorial: Pre-class checklist]{.anchor-tag}

Two things from Session 6's "For Wednesday" must be done:

- **Cowork installed and signed in.**
- **`~/github/aem7010-ai` created locally and pushed to GitHub.**

The first ten minutes of class are for stragglers. If you are stuck, say so immediately. Do not try to catch up silently.

::: {.note-box}
Setup block is in the Session 6 tutorial, "For Wednesday" → item 2. Five-minute job.
:::

## Quick Recap: Session 6

[§ Tutorial: Where we are]{.anchor-tag}

Session 6: **chat**. Probabilistic pattern-completer, copy-paste interface. No file access. No code execution.

**Mode A vs Mode B:**

- **Mode A:** AI *does* the thing. Output is data. Reasoning hidden. → Almost always wrong for research.
- **Mode B:** AI *writes code that does* the thing. Output is a script. Reasoning visible. → The reproducible path.

Chat forced Mode B by accident. **Cowork does not.** Every prompt we write today bakes Mode B in explicitly.

## What Changes When the Agent Can Reach Your Files {.smaller}

[§ Tutorial: What changes]{.anchor-tag}

Three capabilities shift at once.

- **File access.** The agent sees every file in the folder you grant. You no longer paste snippets. You no longer choose what it sees. Granting a folder is a trust decision.
- **Code execution.** R, Python, shell, in a **sandboxed shell** — an isolated terminal on the agent's side, scoped to your folder.
- **On-disk modifications.** The agent creates, edits, and deletes files. Without asking each time, by design.

All three are upgrades. All three are new surfaces for silent damage.

::: {.note-box}
**The guardrail is Git.** `git status` and `git diff` are your only reliable audit trail of what the agent actually did.
:::

## Git as the Safety Net

[§ Tutorial: Git as the safety net]{.anchor-tag}

Every Cowork session in this course follows the same skeleton:

```bash
# Before
cd ~/github/aem7010-ai
mkdir -p session7 && cd session7
git status            # clean

# ... you run Cowork ...

# After
git status            # review what it touched
git diff              # read every line
git add <files to keep>
git commit -m "Session 7: <what you did>"
```

The chat transcript describes what Cowork **claims** it did. Git tells you what it **actually** did. If they disagree, Git wins.

## Confirm the Repo, Grant Permissions

[§ Tutorial: Confirm repo · Cowork permissions]{.anchor-tag}

**30-second check** that last Monday's setup held:

```bash
cd ~/github/aem7010-ai
ls                    # README.md, .gitignore
git status            # clean
```

**Then, Cowork on the floor.** Launch it. Grant access to `~/github/aem7010-ai`. Confirm three things:

1. You can see the folder path in Cowork's UI.
2. You can see the chat transcript beside the file tree.
3. You have found the **stop** button *before* you need it.

::: {.tutorial-cue}
⟶ Switch to the tutorial: **Confirm the AI-module repo** and **Cowork permissions, on the floor** (~5 min).
:::

## Chat and Cowork, Side by Side {.smaller}

[§ Tutorial: Chat and Cowork, side by side]{.anchor-tag}

Same underlying model. Different surface, capabilities, risks.

| Dimension | Chat (Session 6) | Cowork (today) |
|---|---|---|
| Context | Manual paste | Automatic from folder |
| Read files? | No | Yes |
| Run code? | No | Yes, sandboxed |
| Edit files? | No | Yes, directly |
| Model "knows" | Only what you typed | Full files, real errors |
| Audit trail | Git commit of pasted code | Git diff of touched folder |
| Friction | Copy-paste fatigue | Permission + trust decisions |

Capability goes up. Friction goes down. **Risk goes up.** Git turns that risk from catastrophic to annoying.

## What Cowork Is Good At / Bad At {.smaller}

[§ Tutorial: What Cowork is good at · What Cowork is bad at]{.anchor-tag}

**Good:**

- Exploratory data work (real columns, real distributions)
- Multi-step tasks with intermediate state (scrape → clean → plot)
- Debugging with full context ("run this, fix the error")
- Boilerplate (scaffolding, `.gitignore`, `renv` setup)

**Bad:**

- Outputs you cannot sanity-check by eye (an elasticity estimate appears: is it right?)
- Tasks that must run unattended (no cron jobs)
- Work whose reasoning must survive the session (classify 200 rows, save only the CSV → the rule is gone)
- Anything where a confident-sounding hallucination would cost you (fake `rvest` functions, untested selectors)

## The Shared Prompt

[§ Tutorial: Shared prompt handout]{.anchor-tag}

We all paste the **same prompt**. Four design choices matter:

- **Output schema specified exactly** (column names, order, separator)
- **Selector strategy given** (anchor on heading text, not CSS class)
- **Artifact declared** ("the R script is what I care about; do not paste the data")
- **Scope closed** ("do not create other files; do not edit my `.gitignore`")

::: {.note-box}
Mode B enforcement is load-bearing. Do not paraphrase the prompt.
:::

::: {.tutorial-cue}
⟶ Switch to the tutorial: **Shared prompt handout**. Read it before pasting.
:::

## Hands-On: Guided Scrape (~20 min)

[§ Tutorial: Your turn (hands-on) · Verification checklist]{.anchor-tag}

Paste the prompt. Let Cowork write `scrape_dyson_cowork.R`. Do **not** trust the row count it reports.

**Five-step verification checklist** (all must pass before commit):

1. Script exists at `session7/scrape_dyson_cowork.R`?
2. `Rscript scrape_dyson_cowork.R` runs cleanly from a fresh session?
3. CSV has ≥ 80 rows and the 4 expected columns in the right order?
4. Three random rows match the live page?
5. You can explain every line?

If a check fails, ask Cowork for a **patch**, not a full rewrite.

::: {.tutorial-cue}
⟶ Switch to the tutorial: **Your turn (hands-on)** (~20 min). Commit when all five checks pass.
:::

## Stress Test: Three Silent Failures {.smaller}

[§ Tutorial: Stress test]{.anchor-tag}

Add one column: `is_postdoc`, `country`, or `placement_sector`. Ask Cowork. Rerun. Verify **again**.

Three common silent failures:

- **Silent row loss.** Parsing chokes on an unexpected pattern, drops rows. 93 → 90. Nobody notices.
- **Silent row gain.** Agent rewrites parsing, stops filtering blank rows. 93 → 95.
- **Silent column reordering.** You asked for the new column at the end. Agent put it in the middle. Downstream code that expected column 3 to be `placement` now reads the new column. **The most pernicious failure in agent-driven data work.**

::: {.note-box}
**Time-boxed.** If the main exercise ran long, skip this slide's hands-on and just name the three failures out loud.
:::

## What Session 6 Forced, What Cowork Allows {.smaller}

[§ Tutorial: Debrief]{.anchor-tag}

The central lesson of the module is not about a tool. It is about noticing when a tool's constraints were protecting you, and replacing them with discipline when they fall away.

| Behavior | Session 6 (chat) | Session 7 (Cowork) |
|---|---|---|
| Running the script | You had to | The agent can, so you have to *choose* to |
| Verifying the output | You had to | The agent reports; you have to *insist* |
| Saving an artifact | Only as a pasted script | As script *or* displayed table; Mode B is your rule |
| Editing files | You, one paste at a time | The agent, many files, possibly silently |
| Ending the session | Clean by default | Requires `git diff` to know what happened |

**The more capable the tool, the more of the verification reflex you own directly.**

## What's Next

**Next Monday (Session 8): Claude Code.** Terminal-native agent, git-aware, scales to multi-file projects.

**For Monday:**

1. **Push today's repo to GitHub** so your Session 7 work is backed up.
2. Any install steps for Claude Code will be posted on the course site by Saturday. Ten minutes at the start of Monday for troubleshooting.

::: {.note-box}
**The bottleneck is no longer typing. It is verification.** Faster tools do not remove verification work; they move it to the end of the pipeline, where it is easier to skip.
:::

Companion site: [arielortizbobea.github.io/aem7010-practical-skills](https://arielortizbobea.github.io/aem7010-practical-skills/)
